meta.certificationName: Databricks Certified Associate Developer for Apache Spark
meta.title: Mock Exam 4

questions.stem: Which statement best describes lazy evaluation in Spark?

options.text: Spark executes each transformation as soon as it is defined.
Spark waits to build an execution plan until an action is called.
Spark delays only SQL queries, not DataFrame transformations.
Spark delays execution only when caching is enabled.

questions.explanation: B is correct because transformations are evaluated lazily and execute when an action triggers a job. A is false because transformations are not executed immediately. C and D are too narrow; lazy evaluation applies generally, not only to SQL or cached data.
questions.reference: Databricks Certified Associate Developer for Apache Spark Exam Guide (Oct 30, 2025) — Section 1: Apache Spark Architecture and Components

questions.stem: What typically creates a stage boundary in a Spark job?

options.text: A narrow transformation such as filter()
A shuffle caused by a wide transformation such as groupBy()
Calling printSchema()
Referencing a broadcast variable

questions.explanation: B is correct because wide transformations require data exchange across partitions, which creates shuffle boundaries and new stages. A stays within a stage. C is metadata inspection. D does not itself create a stage boundary.
questions.reference: Databricks Certified Associate Developer for Apache Spark Exam Guide (Oct 30, 2025) — Section 1: execution hierarchy, shuffles, and partitions

questions.stem: Why would a developer cache a DataFrame?

options.text: To force all future writes to use Delta format
To avoid recomputing the same lineage when the data is reused
To permanently store the data after the cluster terminates
To increase the number of shuffle partitions automatically

questions.explanation: B is correct because caching keeps reused intermediate results available for later actions. A, C, and D describe unrelated behaviors; caching is not a file-format, persistence-after-cluster, or shuffle-partition setting.
questions.reference: Databricks Certified Associate Developer for Apache Spark Exam Guide (Oct 30, 2025) — Section 1: caching and storage levels

questions.stem: Which variable type is designed to efficiently share a read-only lookup table with all executors?

options.text: Accumulator
Broadcast variable
Temporary view
Checkpoint

questions.explanation: B is correct because broadcast variables distribute read-only data efficiently to executors. Accumulators are for aggregated updates, temporary views expose SQL names, and checkpoints are for fault tolerance and lineage truncation.
questions.reference: Databricks Certified Associate Developer for Apache Spark Exam Guide (Oct 30, 2025) — Section 3: broadcast variables and broadcast joins

questions.stem: What is the primary purpose of an accumulator in Spark?

options.text: To send large lookup data from the driver to executors
To keep a mutable value on workers and read it reliably from executors
To aggregate information such as counters back to the driver
To replace shuffle operations during joins

questions.explanation: C is correct because accumulators are commonly used for counters and aggregated metrics sent back to the driver. A describes broadcast variables. B is misleading because executors should not depend on reading accumulator values for logic. D is unrelated.
questions.reference: Databricks Certified Associate Developer for Apache Spark Exam Guide (Oct 30, 2025) — Section 3: accumulators

questions.stem: Which object is the entry point for working with DataFrames and Spark SQL in PySpark?

options.text: SparkContext
SparkSession
SQLContext
StreamingContext

questions.explanation: B is correct because SparkSession is the unified entry point for DataFrames and SQL APIs. SparkContext is lower level, SQLContext is legacy, and StreamingContext is tied to older streaming APIs.
questions.reference: Databricks Certified Associate Developer for Apache Spark Exam Guide (Oct 30, 2025) — Section 1: SparkSession lifecycle

questions.stem: How does Spark most commonly recover from a lost partition of intermediate data?

options.text: By replaying lineage to recompute the missing partition
By restoring the entire cluster from a snapshot
By converting the DataFrame to pandas and rerunning locally
By forcing all tasks to restart from the first transformation

questions.explanation: A is correct because Spark fault tolerance is based on lineage and partition recomputation. B and C are not standard Spark recovery mechanisms. D is too broad because Spark normally recomputes only what is needed.
questions.reference: Databricks Certified Associate Developer for Apache Spark Exam Guide (Oct 30, 2025) — Section 1: fault tolerance

questions.stem: What does setting spark.sql.shuffle.partitions control?

options.text: The default number of partitions used for shuffle operations
The number of executors that Spark must create
The maximum number of columns in a DataFrame
The default storage level used by cache()

questions.explanation: A is correct because the setting controls the default partition count for shuffle-heavy operations such as joins and aggregations. B, C, and D describe unrelated settings.
questions.reference: Adaptive query execution | Databricks Docs

questions.stem: A developer wants to query a DataFrame with SQL syntax only for the current Spark session. What should they create?

options.text: A temporary view
A broadcast variable
A checkpoint directory
An accumulator

questions.explanation: A is correct because a temporary view registers the DataFrame for SQL use during the current session. The other options are not SQL registration mechanisms.
questions.reference: Databricks Certified Associate Developer for Apache Spark Exam Guide (Oct 30, 2025) — Section 2: temporary views

questions.stem: Which code writes df to Parquet, partitions by country, and replaces existing output?

options.text: df.write.mode("append").partitionBy("country").parquet("/out")
df.write.partitionBy("country").parquet("/out")
df.write.mode("overwrite").partitionBy("country").parquet("/out")
df.write.mode("overwrite").parquet("/out")

questions.explanation: C is correct because it combines overwrite mode with partitionBy on the requested column. A appends. B uses the default save mode. D overwrites but does not partition by country.
questions.reference: Databricks Certified Associate Developer for Apache Spark Exam Guide (Oct 30, 2025) — Sample Question 1

questions.stem: Which statement best matches the exam objective about querying files directly with Spark SQL?

options.text: Spark SQL can query supported file-based data sources such as JSON, CSV, ORC, text, and Delta files.
Spark SQL can query only managed tables, not files.
Spark SQL can query files only after they are converted to pandas.
Spark SQL can query CSV files but not JSON or Delta files.

questions.explanation: A is correct and matches the official exam guide. B, C, and D contradict the guide’s stated scope for direct file querying.
questions.reference: Databricks Certified Associate Developer for Apache Spark Exam Guide (Oct 30, 2025) — Section 2: Execute SQL queries directly on files

questions.stem: Which write operation is most appropriate when the goal is to save query results as a persistent table for later SQL use?

options.text: df.createOrReplaceTempView("sales")
df.write.saveAsTable("sales")
df.collect()
df.printSchema()

questions.explanation: B is correct because saveAsTable writes data as a persistent table. A creates only a session-scoped view. C and D do not persist query results.
questions.reference: Databricks Certified Associate Developer for Apache Spark Exam Guide (Oct 30, 2025) — Section 2: save data to persistent tables

questions.stem: When reading from a JDBC source, which option usually identifies the remote table or query to load?

options.text: checkpointLocation
pathGlobFilter
dbtable
mergeSchema

questions.explanation: C is correct because dbtable is the common JDBC option used to name the table or subquery to read. The other options are used for different features and data sources.
questions.reference: Spark API options reference | Databricks Docs

questions.stem: Why might a developer provide an explicit schema when reading CSV data?

options.text: To avoid inference mistakes and make the read more predictable
To force Spark to create exactly one partition
To disable lazy evaluation
To make the DataFrame automatically cached

questions.explanation: A is correct because explicitly defining schema improves control and avoids incorrect inference. B, C, and D are unrelated to schema specification.
questions.reference: Databricks Certified Associate Developer for Apache Spark Exam Guide (Oct 30, 2025) — Section 3: reading DataFrames with schemas

questions.stem: Which operation renames an existing DataFrame column?

options.text: withColumnRenamed()
renamePartition()
aliasTable()
toDFSchema()

questions.explanation: A is correct because withColumnRenamed() changes a column name in the resulting DataFrame. The other methods are not valid DataFrame column-renaming APIs for this task.
questions.reference: Databricks Certified Associate Developer for Apache Spark Exam Guide (Oct 30, 2025) — Section 3: manipulate columns and table structures

questions.stem: A column named tags contains an array of strings. Which transformation creates one output row per array element?

options.text: explode("tags")
split("tags")
collect_list("tags")
drop("tags")

questions.explanation: A is correct because explode turns each array element into its own row. split is for turning strings into arrays, collect_list aggregates rows into arrays, and drop removes the column.
questions.reference: Databricks Certified Associate Developer for Apache Spark Exam Guide (Oct 30, 2025) — Section 3: exploding arrays

questions.stem: Which method removes duplicate rows based on one or more selected columns?

options.text: distinctPartitions()
dropDuplicates()
dedupeRows()
uniqueBy()

questions.explanation: B is correct because dropDuplicates() supports deduplication with an optional subset of columns. The other options are not the standard API for this task.
questions.reference: Databricks Certified Associate Developer for Apache Spark Exam Guide (Oct 30, 2025) — Section 3: data deduplication

questions.stem: A dashboard needs a fast estimate of unique users and can tolerate a small error margin. Which function is the best fit?

options.text: count()
sumDistinct()
approx_count_distinct()
collect_set()

questions.explanation: C is correct because approx_count_distinct() trades a small amount of precision for better scalability and speed on large datasets. The other options do not directly solve the stated unique-count requirement as well.
questions.reference: Databricks Certified Associate Developer for Apache Spark Exam Guide (Oct 30, 2025) — Sample Question 10

questions.stem: A column event_ts stores Unix epoch seconds. What kind of transformation is typically needed before extracting year or month values?

options.text: Convert the epoch value to a date or timestamp representation
Broadcast the epoch column to all executors
Register the column as a temporary view
Cache the DataFrame with DISK_ONLY

questions.explanation: A is correct because date-part extraction usually requires first converting Unix epoch values into date or timestamp form. The other actions do not address date manipulation.
questions.reference: Databricks Certified Associate Developer for Apache Spark Exam Guide (Oct 30, 2025) — Section 3: manipulate Date data type

questions.stem: When joining a very large DataFrame to a much smaller lookup DataFrame, why might a broadcast join help?

options.text: It avoids sending the large DataFrame across the network for a full shuffle when the small side can be broadcast.
It guarantees the join result is sorted by the join key.
It converts the large DataFrame into a pandas DataFrame before joining.
It removes null values from the join columns automatically.

questions.explanation: A is correct because broadcasting the small side can reduce shuffle cost. B, C, and D are not guarantees or behaviors of broadcast joins.
questions.reference: Databricks Certified Associate Developer for Apache Spark Exam Guide (Oct 30, 2025) — Section 3: broadcast joins

questions.stem: Which join type returns only rows whose keys exist in both input DataFrames?

options.text: left
inner
cross
outer

questions.explanation: B is correct because an inner join keeps only matching keys from both sides. Left, cross, and outer joins have broader result sets.
questions.reference: Databricks Certified Associate Developer for Apache Spark Exam Guide (Oct 30, 2025) — Section 3: combining DataFrames with joins

questions.stem: Which union behavior is safest when two DataFrames have the same columns but possibly in different orders?

options.text: Use an approach that aligns columns by name instead of relying only on position.
Always use cross join first.
Convert both DataFrames to RDDs to avoid schema issues.
Call collect() on both sides before combining.

questions.explanation: A is correct because aligning by name avoids accidental column-position mismatches. B, C, and D are unnecessary or incorrect for safe DataFrame union behavior.
questions.reference: Databricks Certified Associate Developer for Apache Spark Exam Guide (Oct 30, 2025) — Section 3: union and union all

questions.stem: Which operation sorts a DataFrame by one or more columns?

options.text: orderBy()
groupBy()
withWatermark()
persistTable()

questions.explanation: A is correct because orderBy() or sort() performs DataFrame sorting. The other operations are for aggregation, streaming watermarking, or nonstandard behavior.
questions.reference: Databricks Certified Associate Developer for Apache Spark Exam Guide (Oct 30, 2025) — Section 3: sorting

questions.stem: What does printSchema() provide?

options.text: A textual view of the DataFrame schema and nested structure
A count of all rows in the DataFrame
A cached copy of the DataFrame
A SQL temporary view

questions.explanation: A is correct because printSchema() shows columns, data types, and nesting. It does not count rows, cache data, or register SQL views.
questions.reference: Databricks Certified Associate Developer for Apache Spark Exam Guide (Oct 30, 2025) — Section 3: printing schema

questions.stem: What is returned by collect() on a DataFrame?

options.text: A list of Row objects on the driver
A new streaming DataFrame
A persistent table in the metastore
A broadcast variable

questions.explanation: A is correct because collect() materializes the result as Row objects on the driver. The other answers describe different Spark concepts.
questions.reference: Databricks Certified Associate Developer for Apache Spark Exam Guide (Oct 30, 2025) — Section 3: conversion between DataFrame and sequence/list formats

questions.stem: According to Databricks guidance, when should built-in Spark functions usually be preferred over a Python UDF?

options.text: When the logic can be expressed with built-in functions and the workload runs regularly or at scale
Only when the DataFrame has fewer than 100 rows
Only for temporary views
Only inside Spark Connect sessions

questions.explanation: A is correct because Databricks recommends built-in Spark functions for scalable, optimized distributed processing. The other choices are arbitrary or unrelated.
questions.reference: What are user-defined functions (UDFs)? | Databricks Docs

questions.stem: Which call drops rows that contain at least one null value in any column?

options.text: df.na.drop()
df.na.drop("all")
df.fillna(0)
df.drop("null")

questions.explanation: A is correct because the default behavior of na.drop() removes rows containing nulls. B drops rows only when all values are null. C fills values instead of dropping. D is not a null-handling method.
questions.reference: Databricks Certified Associate Developer for Apache Spark Exam Guide (Oct 30, 2025) — Sample Question 6

questions.stem: A developer needs to keep only rows where age is at least 18. Which transformation best fits?

options.text: filter(col("age") >= 18)
dropDuplicates(["age"])
groupBy("age")
cache()

questions.explanation: A is correct because filter applies row-level predicates for validation or selection. The other operations remove duplicates, aggregate, or cache data instead.
questions.reference: Databricks Certified Associate Developer for Apache Spark Exam Guide (Oct 30, 2025) — Section 3: applying filters and validation

questions.stem: A join must match both customer_id and order_date. What should the developer do?

options.text: Join using both columns as the join condition
Use a cross join and filter later because Spark does not support multiple-key joins
Convert both DataFrames to pandas before joining
Broadcast both DataFrames regardless of size

questions.explanation: A is correct because Spark supports joins on multiple keys directly. B is inefficient and incorrect, C is unnecessary, and D ignores data size and join planning.
questions.reference: Databricks Certified Associate Developer for Apache Spark Exam Guide (Oct 30, 2025) — Section 3: joins on multiple keys

questions.stem: A developer needs custom row-level logic in a streaming job, but the logic must not depend on prior events or maintained history. What is the best fit?

options.text: A UDF used without stateful operators
A stream-stream join with state retention
A running window aggregate across all prior rows
A checkpoint-only sink with complete mode

questions.explanation: A is correct because stateless custom logic is a good fit for a UDF without stateful operators. B and C require maintained state. D describes output/checkpoint behavior, not the transformation logic itself.
questions.reference: Databricks Certified Associate Developer for Apache Spark Exam Guide (Oct 30, 2025) — Sample Question 9

questions.stem: What is a common reason to inspect summary statistics such as mean or summary() results during development?

options.text: To understand data distribution and quickly validate aggregate behavior
To create a temporary view automatically
To disable shuffles for future jobs
To convert the DataFrame into a broadcast variable

questions.explanation: A is correct because aggregate summaries help validate data and computations. B, C, and D are unrelated effects.
questions.reference: Databricks Certified Associate Developer for Apache Spark Exam Guide (Oct 30, 2025) — Section 3: aggregate operations such as mean and summary

questions.stem: When reducing the number of partitions after a large filter, which operation is often chosen to avoid a full reshuffle when possible?

options.text: coalesce()
dropDuplicates()
explode()
checkpoint()

questions.explanation: A is correct because coalesce() is commonly used to reduce partitions with less data movement than repartition(). The other methods solve different problems.
questions.reference: Databricks Certified Associate Developer for Apache Spark Exam Guide (Oct 30, 2025) — Section 4: repartitioning and coalescing

questions.stem: Which capability is a documented benefit of Adaptive Query Execution (AQE)?

options.text: Dynamically switching some sort-merge joins to broadcast hash joins at runtime
Converting every DataFrame into a cached table before execution
Disabling all shuffles in SQL queries
Guaranteeing that all jobs use exactly 200 partitions

questions.explanation: A is correct because AQE can re-optimize plans at runtime, including join strategy changes. B, C, and D are not AQE guarantees.
questions.reference: Adaptive query execution | Databricks Docs

questions.stem: An engineer wants to inspect executor logs for a slow job. Which tool is the official sample guide answer?

options.text: The Spark UI
A pandas DataFrame profile report
The SQL warehouse browser cache
A Delta table history command

questions.explanation: A is correct because the official sample question points to using the Spark UI to view executor logs. The other options are not the stated troubleshooting path.
questions.reference: Databricks Certified Associate Developer for Apache Spark Exam Guide (Oct 30, 2025) — Sample Question 2

questions.stem: Why is data skew a performance concern in Spark?

options.text: A few partitions can become much larger than others, leaving some tasks straggling while others finish early.
It prevents DataFrames from being registered as temporary views.
It forces Spark to use local mode only.
It makes all joins broadcast joins.

questions.explanation: A is correct because skew creates uneven work distribution and slow tail tasks. B, C, and D are unrelated to skew.
questions.reference: Databricks Certified Associate Developer for Apache Spark Exam Guide (Oct 30, 2025) — Section 4: identifying data skew

questions.stem: What is the main purpose of a checkpoint location in Structured Streaming?

options.text: To store query progress and state needed for fault tolerance and recovery
To convert a streaming query into batch mode
To broadcast a static dimension table
To infer the schema of all incoming files automatically

questions.explanation: A is correct because checkpoints preserve progress and state for restart and exactly-once style processing guarantees. B, C, and D are different concerns.
questions.reference: Structured Streaming concepts | Databricks Docs

questions.stem: Which statement best reflects the Structured Streaming programming model described by Databricks?

options.text: Streaming computations are expressed using familiar Spark APIs, and the engine updates results incrementally as new data arrives.
Streaming requires a completely different language from batch Spark.
Streaming queries cannot use aggregations.
Streaming processing always operates on exactly one record at a time with no batching.

questions.explanation: A is correct because Databricks describes Structured Streaming as using familiar Spark APIs with incremental execution. B, C, and D contradict the official concepts page.
questions.reference: Structured Streaming concepts | Databricks Docs

questions.stem: Why is a watermark useful for streaming deduplication or windowed stateful processing?

options.text: It helps bound how long late data is considered, which helps limit retained state.
It permanently sorts all records by event time before ingestion.
It disables fault tolerance to reduce latency.
It forces the sink to use complete output mode.

questions.explanation: A is correct because watermarks control lateness tolerance and state retention for stateful operations. B, C, and D are incorrect behaviors.
questions.reference: Structured Streaming concepts | Databricks Docs

questions.stem: A team wants updated streaming aggregates every 2 minutes rather than continuously after every tiny arrival. Which setting is most directly relevant?

options.text: Trigger interval
Storage level
Broadcast timeout
Temp view name

questions.explanation: A is correct because trigger intervals control how often micro-batches run and results update. The other settings do not schedule streaming execution cadence.
questions.reference: Structured Streaming concepts | Databricks Docs

questions.stem: Which operation type is explicitly within scope for basic streaming DataFrame work in the official guide?

options.text: Selection, projection, windowing, and aggregation
Only model serving and vector search
Only RDD checkpoint internals
Only batch-only JDBC writes

questions.explanation: A is correct because the guide lists basic streaming operations such as selection, projection, window, and aggregation. The other answers are outside this exam objective.
questions.reference: Databricks Certified Associate Developer for Apache Spark Exam Guide (Oct 30, 2025) — Section 5: Structured Streaming

questions.stem: What is a key benefit of Databricks Connect / Spark Connect style development?

options.text: It lets developers use local tools or IDEs while running Spark work on Databricks compute remotely.
It requires every Spark job to run entirely on the developer laptop.
It replaces DataFrames with only SQL text files.
It disables cluster execution mode.

questions.explanation: A is correct because Databricks Connect enables local development against remote Databricks compute. B, C, and D misstate the feature.
questions.reference: Databricks Connect | Databricks Docs

questions.stem: Which Spark deployment mode runs driver and executors on a single machine?

options.text: Local mode
Cluster mode
Client mode
Serverless mode

questions.explanation: A is correct because local mode runs everything on one machine. Cluster and client modes distribute execution differently, and serverless is not one of the exam guide’s named deployment mode types here.
questions.reference: Databricks Certified Associate Developer for Apache Spark Exam Guide (Oct 30, 2025) — Sample Question 7

questions.stem: In client deployment mode, where does the driver program run?

options.text: On the machine that submitted the application
Always on a worker node chosen by Spark
Inside every executor simultaneously
Only inside a SQL warehouse

questions.explanation: A is correct because in client mode the driver stays with the submitting client. B describes cluster-style behavior, while C and D are incorrect.
questions.reference: Databricks Certified Associate Developer for Apache Spark Exam Guide (Oct 30, 2025) — Section 6: deployment mode types

questions.stem: What is the main advantage of Pandas API on Spark described in Databricks docs?

options.text: It provides pandas-like APIs on top of Spark so familiar workflows can scale beyond local pandas limits.
It guarantees faster execution than every PySpark operation in all cases.
It stores all data only in browser memory.
It removes the need for Spark compute entirely.

questions.explanation: A is correct because Pandas API on Spark brings familiar pandas-style operations to Spark-backed distributed data. B is too absolute, and C and D are false.
questions.reference: Can you use pandas on Databricks? | Databricks Docs

questions.stem: What is a defining characteristic of a pandas UDF?

options.text: It uses pandas with Arrow-optimized data transfer for vectorized execution patterns.
It can run only in local mode.
It always returns exactly one scalar for the whole DataFrame.
It replaces the need for Spark SQL functions in every workload.

questions.explanation: A is correct because Databricks describes pandas UDFs as Arrow-optimized and vectorized. B, C, and D are inaccurate or overly broad.
questions.reference: pandas user-defined functions | Databricks Docs
